fragments.content: Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD) is characterized by excessive, uncontrollable worry about multiple events or activities occurring more days than not for at least 6 months. The anxiety and worry are associated with ≥3 physical/cognitive symptoms: restlessness, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, irritability, muscle tension, or sleep disturbance. Symptoms cause clinically significant distress/impairment and are not attributable to substances, medical conditions, or other mental disorders. GAD has a lifetime prevalence of ~5-6%, with female predominance (2:1). Pathophysiology involves amygdala hyperactivity, prefrontal cortex dysregulation, GABAergic dysfunction, and genetic factors (heritability ~30%).

First-line treatments include SSRIs (escitalopram, sertraline, paroxetine) and SNRIs (venlafaxine, duloxetine). Buspirone is an alternative anxiolytic. Benzodiazepines provide rapid relief but are limited to short-term use due to dependence risk. Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) is equally effective as medication and may have more durable benefits after discontinuation; key components include cognitive restructuring, worry exposure, and relaxation training. Combination therapy (medication + CBT) may benefit severe cases. Treatment duration typically 6-12 months after symptom remission before gradual tapering. Pregabalin shows efficacy in some studies.

DSM-5-TR criteria for GAD: excessive anxiety and worry about multiple events/activities occurring more days than not for ≥6 months. Individual finds it difficult to control the worry. Anxiety/worry associated with ≥3 of: restlessness/keyed up, easily fatigued, mind going blank/difficulty concentrating, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance. Symptoms cause clinically significant distress/impairment in functioning. Disturbance not attributable to substance effects, medical condition, or better explained by another mental disorder (e.g., panic disorder, social anxiety, OCD, PTSD). Specifiers include in partial/full remission.
